title: Disruption of the oak tree microbiome with urbanization

data_scope.study_system: oak tree microbiome across leaves, roots and soils
data_scope.spatial_design: urban-to-rural gradient anchored in Boston, Massachusetts (USA) with rural reference sites
data_scope.exact_sample_counts: not stated in public abstract (trees/sites/reads held in EDI metadata package)

core_conclusion: Urbanization shifts the structure and composition of the oak tree microbiome across leaves, roots and soils, reducing mutualistic root/leaf symbionts and increasing decomposers and pathogens (including plant, animal and human/zoonotic pathogens). The shifts correlate with urban stressors (heat, drought, aerosol deposition) and alter biogeochemistry: higher N2O-production potential and lower methane consumption. Overall microbiome diversity falls, especially among non-pathogens, potentially diminishing the ecological and health benefits of urban trees.
why_it_matters: Shows the invisible microbiome half of urban trees is degraded by urban conditions, so canopy cover overstates delivered benefit; links urban form to greenhouse-gas fluxes (N2O up, methane uptake down); flags a One Health pathogen-exposure signal; and identifies named stressor levers (heat, drought, aerosols) for intervention.
why_nature: Couples a clear urbanization gradient to structural and functional change in a living system; cross-cuts microbial ecology, biogeochemistry/climate and public health (One Health); policy-translatable for urban forestry, green infrastructure and city GHG inventories; delivers mechanism plus downstream consequence.
